retrieved_evidence.text: Laboratory reports should be interpreted using reference ranges, symptoms, prior results, and the ordering context. Hemoglobin, WBC, RBC, platelet count, glucose, creatinine, bilirubin, AST, ALT, CRP, and ESR are common values that may appear in reports. Abnormal lab values can indicate infection, anemia, inflammation, liver injury, kidney dysfunction, or metabolic issues, depending on the pattern. Single values should not be over-interpreted without the full report and clinical context. OCR may misread digits, units, and abbreviations, so manual verification is required before any clinical decision.

Chest X-ray findings such as infiltration, effusion, consolidation, and cardiomegaly may require clinical correlation with symptoms, laboratory results, and physician evaluation. Brain MRI tumor classification outputs such as glioma, meningioma, pituitary tumor, and no tumor are probabilistic model outputs and should not be treated as final diagnosis without radiologist confirmation. OCR-extracted prescriptions and laboratory reports may contain recognition errors. Medication names, doses, and lab values should be checked manually before clinical use. Multimodal health record summarization combines image-based evidence, document-based evidence, and knowledge-base retrieval to produce a more complete patient overview.

Chest X-ray findings should be interpreted with clinical symptoms, history, and laboratory data. Infiltration may reflect infection, inflammation, or other lung parenchymal abnormality. Consolidation can suggest pneumonia, atelectatic change, or another focal air-space process depending on context. Effusion may indicate pleural fluid and should be correlated with cardiopulmonary findings. Cardiomegaly can be associated with cardiac disease, fluid overload, or projectional factors. Automated X-ray outputs are supportive findings and are not a final diagnosis.

Prescriptions should be checked carefully for patient identity, drug name, dose, frequency, and duration. Common prescription abbreviations include tablet, capsule, syrup, injection, mg, ml, daily, bd, tds, and od. Handwritten or scanned prescriptions can contain OCR errors, missing characters, and ambiguous medication names. Medication safety requires checking for contraindications, duplicate therapy, allergies, and age-appropriate dosing. Automated prescription OCR should support documentation and review, not replace pharmacist or physician verification.

Brain MRI tumor labels such as glioma, meningioma, pituitary tumor, and no tumor are probabilistic outputs from a classifier. Glioma often requires correlation with lesion location, edema, mass effect, and contrast enhancement patterns. Meningioma is commonly extra-axial and may show a dural-based mass with adjacent mass effect or calcification. Pituitary tumors may relate to sellar or suprasellar findings and can affect vision or hormone-related symptoms. No tumor does not replace radiology review; image quality, slice coverage, and clinical context still matter. Any automated MRI result should be reviewed by a qualified radiologist before clinical use.
